draft.introduction: Hebrews 12:1–14 calls believers to run the race of faith looking to Jesus, “the founder and perfecter of our faith,” and warns that God disciplines those he loves. From Codex Sinaiticus, fourth century.

translation.text: Therefore, since we are surrounded by so great a cloud of witnesses, let us also lay aside every weight, and sin which clings so closely, and let us run with endurance the race that is set before us, looking to Jesus, the founder and perfecter of our faith, who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross, despising the shame, and is seated at the right hand of the throne of God.
translation.original_text: τοιγαρουν και ημεις τοσουτον εχοντες περικειμενον ημιν νεφος μαρτυρων

translation.text: For they disciplined us for a short time as it seemed best to them, but he disciplines us for our good, that we may share his holiness. For the moment all discipline seems painful rather than pleasant, but later it yields the peaceful fruit of righteousness to those who have been trained by it.
translation.original_text: οι μεν γαρ προς ολιγας ημερας κατα το δοκουν αυτοις επαιδευον

translator_notes.note: The phrase “that we may share his holiness” (εις το μεταλαβειν της αγιοτητος αυτου) is an explicit theosis text: the goal of divine discipline is human participation in God’s own holiness.
